In this chapter, the collision between worship and rejection, faith and hardness of heart, reaches a terrible clarity. Mary anoints Jesus' feet with expensive perfume—a gesture of devotion that costs her dearly—while Judas demands to know why the money was not given to the poor. John unmasks Judas's hypocrisy: he does not care about the poor but is a thief. Yet Jesus defends Mary's extravagance without dismissing the poor. Instead, He speaks something far more unsettling: "The poor you will always have with you, but you will not always have Me." This is not callousness toward poverty. This is the weight of a present reality that demands response. Mary understood what was coming. She prepared Jesus for His burial because she alone, in that moment, truly saw Him. The question hanging over this chapter becomes inescapable: Do you see? Will you respond while you still can?

The palm branches and hosannas of Jesus' entry into Jerusalem create an irony so sharp it cuts. The crowds greet Him as King, shouting words from Psalm 118—"Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord!" Yet they do not understand what they are witnessing. They have come because of the sign of Lazarus raised from death, and the Pharisees watch in helpless rage as the whole world seems to follow Him. But following and understanding are not the same. The crowds see the miracle; they miss the meaning. Their darkness is not innocent. After performing so many signs, Jesus Himself says, they still do not believe. John quotes Isaiah to explain this hardness: some have eyes that see but do not perceive, ears that hear but do not understand. God Himself has hardened their hearts. This is doctrine that disturbs us because it should. It means that not all seeing leads to believing, and not all hearing leads to obedience. Proximity to Jesus is not salvation.

The heart of the chapter comes in Jesus' response to the simple request: Greeks want to see Jesus. This simple longing triggers His declaration that "the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified." Glorification, He explains, comes through death—like a kernel of wheat that must fall to the ground and die to bear much fruit. This is glory redefined. This is victory explained through loss. Jesus does not hide from what is coming. His soul is troubled. Yet He does not ask the Father to save Him from the hour; instead, He asks the Father to glorify His name through it. The voice from heaven confirms this—"I have glorified it, and I will glorify it again." Most in the crowd hear only thunder. They miss the Father's affirmation of the Son's sacrifice. Only those listening can hear God speaking.

Yet here is where John leaves us stripped bare: Jesus says the Light is among them for a little while longer. Walk while you have the Light. Believe in the Light. Become sons of light. The opportunity is present but not permanent. Some leaders believe but remain silent, loving praise from men more than the praise of God—a choice that reveals the deepest truth about us all. Will we love the approval of the world more than the truth of Christ? Jesus makes His final claim plain: to believe in Him is to believe in the One who sent Him. To see Him is to see the Father. He came as Light so that believers would not remain in darkness. The Word He speaks will judge those who reject it on the last day. There is no neutral ground. You are either walking in the Light or walking in darkness. You cannot see where darkness leads until you are already lost in it.
